FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes ABCD Study data; methods state "sample from the baseline assessment of the ABCD 2.0.1 release in 2019"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis comparing video gamers (VGs) and non-video gamers (NVGs) with fMRI and behavioral outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in JAMA Network Open

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposure variable is generic "video gaming hours per week" with no platform specification (unspecified-platform gaming). Methods state "participants completed a self-reported screen time survey including an item asking children to report the time specifically spent on video gaming." No phone/SM-specific estimate. Step 2 composite: not applicable as no composite is analyzed. Binding clarification: "Gaming alone (VGAQ, IGDS9-SF, console, PC, unspecified-platform, 'gaming addiction') fails IC2 unless explicitly mobile/phone-based OR paired with separately-measured phone/SM variable."

notes: Paper compares VGs vs NVGs on cognitive performance (fMRI BOLD signal, response inhibition, working memory). While cognitive outcomes are individual-trait-level, the exposure (generic gaming time, platform unspecified) fails the phone/SM-specific requirement per binding IC2 rules.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes ABCD Study data; methods state "longitudinal, demographically diverse cohort of more than 3,000 young adolescents participating in the ongoing Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study in the United States"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using machine learning models to predict psychological distress from pandemic survey data

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found. Features include generic "screen time" but no coefficient for "time on social media," "texting," "smartphone use," etc. Step 2: Composite check - paper reports top 20 screen time features including "watching news related to coronavirus," "playing single-player video games," and "browsing the internet" (Figures 3-4). These are bundled without phone/SM isolation. Binding clarification: "Multi-device aggregated time fails Step 2. Any non-phone/non-SM ingredient (TV, console gaming, PC gaming, unspecified gaming, music, reading on screens, computer-only) without separate breakout → NOT_MET."

notes: Paper uses ML to predict mental health outcomes (positive affect, stress, anxiety, depressive symptoms) from pandemic predictors including screen time. However, screen time composite includes non-phone/SM items (gaming, internet browsing, news) bundled without breakout, failing Step 2 IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes ABCD Study data; methods state "functional magnetic resonance imaging data from 6758 individuals in the Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development (ABCD) study wave 1 release"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using latent profile analysis to identify neurodevelopmental subgroups

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Biological Psychiatry: Cognitive Neuroscience and Neuroimaging

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable analyzed as IV or DV with reported coefficient. Step 2: Screen time mentioned only as a descriptive characteristic of profiles: "more screen time (F = 6.80, p < .0001)" without methods describing composite items. Critically, IC2 requires a phone/SM variable paired with an individual-trait outcome in an inferential analysis. Here, screen time is a profile correlate but not the focus of an SM↔individual-trait analysis. Paper is profile description, not SM association testing.

notes: Paper validates latent profile solutions in ABCD sample, relating profiles to clinical features including screen time. However, no inferential analysis of smartphone/SM use paired with individual-trait outcome. Screen time is reported descriptively as a profile characteristic, not analyzed as IV or DV.
